title: New York General Business Law Article 47 (Artificial Intelligence Companion Models)
summary: This regulation establishes mandatory safety and transparency protocols for operators of 'AI companions'—systems designed to simulate human-like relationships through emotional recognition and personalized interaction. It applies to any business entity providing such AI systems to users in New York for personal use. The law specifically mandates the implementation of suicide prevention protocols and clear disclosures about the non-human nature of the interaction.
key_requirements: Implement a protocol to take reasonable efforts for detecting and addressing suicidal ideation or expressions of self-harm (Section 1701).
Provide a notification to the user referring them to crisis service providers (e.g., 9-8-8 hotline) upon detection of self-harm expressions (Section 1701).
Provide a clear and conspicuous notification at the beginning of any AI companion interaction stating the user is not communicating with a human (Section 1702).
Repeat the human-disclosure notification at least every three hours for continuing interactions (Section 1702).
Maintain documentation of safety protocol implementation and notification delivery for regulatory inspection (Section 1703).
compliance_implications: Maximum civil penalty of $15,000 per day for violations of safety or notification requirements. Enforced by the New York Attorney General (Section 1703).
All fines and penalties collected are deposited into the state suicide prevention fund (Section 1703).
Requires ongoing monitoring of user interactions for specific safety triggers, necessitating robust natural language processing (NLP) capabilities.
Mandates specific UI/UX design changes to ensure 'clear and conspicuous' disclosures are presented at required intervals.

title: California AB 2013 (Generative Artificial Intelligence: Training Data Transparency Act)
summary: This law requires developers of generative AI systems or services made available to Californians to publicly disclose high-level summaries of the data used for training. It applies to any AI system released or substantially modified on or after January 1, 2022. The disclosure must be posted on the developer's website and include details on data sources, types of data points, and whether the data is copyrighted or contains personal information.
key_requirements: Post documentation on the developer's website regarding the data used to train the generative AI system or service (Civil Code Section 3111).
Provide a high-level summary of datasets, including sources, owners, and a description of how they further the AI's intended purpose (Section 3111(a)).
Disclose the number of data points in datasets, which may be provided in general ranges (Section 3111(a)(3)).
State whether the datasets include personal information or aggregate consumer information as defined by the CCPA (Section 3111(a)(7-8)).
Disclose whether the system used or continuously uses synthetic data generation in its development (Section 3111(a)(12)).
Identify whether datasets include data protected by copyright, trademark, or patent (Section 3111(a)(5)).
compliance_implications: Maximum civil penalty of $5,000 per violation, with each day of non-compliance deemed a separate violation. Enforced by the California Attorney General (Civil Code Section 17206 via UCL).
Requires developers to maintain detailed records of data provenance and processing for all models released since 2022.
Mandates public-facing documentation that must be updated whenever a 'substantial modification' (retraining or fine-tuning) occurs.
Potential for private right of action under California's Unfair Competition Law (UCL) for failure to disclose required information.

title: EU AI Act Code of Practice for General-Purpose AI (GPAI)
summary: The GPAI Code of Practice is the primary compliance tool for providers of general-purpose AI models to demonstrate adherence to the EU AI Act's obligations. It covers transparency, copyright, and safety/security requirements, with specific focus on models with systemic risk. While voluntary, signing the code provides a 'presumption of conformity' with the AI Act, significantly reducing administrative burdens for providers.
key_requirements: Establish a policy to respect Union copyright law, including a summary of content used for training (Article 53).
Maintain technical documentation for the AI Office and national competent authorities (Article 53).
Provide information and documentation to downstream providers who integrate the GPAI model into their own AI systems (Article 53).
Conduct model evaluations, including adversarial testing, for models with systemic risk (Article 55).
Implement a risk management framework to identify and mitigate systemic risks at the Union level (Article 55).
Report serious incidents and possible corrective measures to the AI Office without undue delay (Article 55).
compliance_implications: Non-compliance with the underlying AI Act obligations can result in fines up to 35 million EUR or 7% of total worldwide annual turnover, whichever is higher. Enforced by the EU AI Office.
Signatories benefit from a streamlined compliance process and legal certainty regarding the interpretation of 'state-of-the-art' safety measures.
Requires significant investment in internal auditing, adversarial testing (red-teaming), and documentation management.
Mandates ongoing cooperation with the AI Office, including participation in the Signatory Taskforce for continuous improvement of the Code.

title: South Korea Act on the Development of Artificial Intelligence and Establishment of Trust (AI Basic Act)
summary: This comprehensive national law regulates the development and use of AI in South Korea, focusing on 'high-impact AI' and 'high-performance AI'. It applies to both developers and utilizers of AI systems that affect the Korean market, including extraterritorial providers. The Act mandates transparency for AI-generated content and imposes strict safety and explainability requirements on AI used in critical sectors like healthcare and hiring.
key_requirements: Provide clear notice that content is AI-generated for sound, images, or video difficult to distinguish from human-created content.
Assess whether an AI system qualifies as 'high-impact AI' (e.g., healthcare, hiring, biometric analysis) before deployment.
Provide a 'meaningful explanation' of high-impact AI outcomes, including key criteria and a summary of training data.
Implement a user protection plan and a mechanism for human oversight for all high-impact AI systems.
Designate a local representative for foreign AI businesses meeting revenue or user thresholds to handle safety reports and inquiries.
Implement a risk management plan for 'high-performance AI' (trained with >10^26 FLOPs) and report implementation to MSIT.
compliance_implications: Administrative fines of up to 30 million KRW (approx. $21,000) for failing to notify users, failing to appoint a representative, or violating corrective orders. Enforced by MSIT.
MSIT may issue corrective orders, including the suspension of services that pose a threat to public safety.
Requires foreign providers to establish a legal presence or agency in South Korea, increasing operational costs.
Mandates 'impact assessments' on fundamental rights before incorporating high-impact AI into products or services.
source_name: Ministry of Science and ICT (South Korea)

title: ISO/IEC 42006:2025 (Requirements for bodies providing audit and certification of AI management systems)
summary: ISO/IEC 42006 specifies requirements for certification bodies that perform audits and certification of AI Management Systems (AIMS) according to ISO/IEC 42001. It ensures that AI audits are conducted with the necessary technical competence, consistency, and impartiality. The standard is a critical component of the global AI governance ecosystem, enabling organizations to demonstrate verified compliance with international best practices.
key_requirements: Establish competence requirements for audit teams, including specific knowledge of AI technologies, ethics, and risk assessment (Clause 7).
Conduct a two-stage audit process to evaluate the organization's AIMS design and its practical implementation (Clause 9).
Verify the organization's processes for identifying and managing AI-specific risks throughout the system lifecycle (Clause 9).
Evaluate the effectiveness of controls for data quality, bias mitigation, and transparency in AI outputs (Clause 9).
Maintain impartiality and manage conflicts of interest in the certification process (Clause 5).
compliance_implications: No direct legal enforcement; however, certification is increasingly required in government procurement and high-risk industry contracts.
Organizations seeking certification must undergo rigorous third-party audits, requiring extensive documentation of their AI governance processes.
Provides a globally recognized benchmark for 'responsible AI', reducing the need for multiple jurisdiction-specific audits.
Failure to maintain AIMS standards can lead to the suspension or withdrawal of certification, impacting market access and reputation.

title: NIST AI 800-1 (Managing Misuse Risk for Dual-Use Foundation Models)
summary: NIST AI 800-1 provides a voluntary framework for developers of large-scale 'dual-use' foundation models to identify and mitigate risks of misuse that could threaten national security or public safety. It outlines best practices for safety testing, including red-teaming for chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear (CBRN) risks, as well as cyberattack capabilities.
key_requirements: Conduct comprehensive safety testing and red-teaming to identify model capabilities that could be misused for harmful purposes.
Implement 'safety-by-design' principles throughout the model development lifecycle, including data filtering and alignment techniques.
Establish a risk management plan that includes specific thresholds for 'unacceptable risk' and corresponding mitigation actions.
Provide transparent reporting on safety test results and residual risks to the US AI Safety Institute (AISI).
Implement robust access controls and monitoring to prevent unauthorized access to powerful model weights or specialized fine-tuning capabilities.
compliance_implications: Voluntary for private industry, but compliance is often a prerequisite for federal contracts and is closely monitored by the Department of Commerce.
Requires significant technical resources for advanced red-teaming and safety evaluation.
Establishes a 'duty of care' standard that may be referenced in future enforcement actions or civil litigation regarding AI-related harms.
Mandates ongoing collaboration with the US AI Safety Institute for the development of new safety benchmarks and testing methodologies.
